exemplar.title: How does increasing horizontal drag forces affect the range of a projectile motion?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does increasing horizontal drag forces affect the range of a projectile motion?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are communicated both clearly and precisely, with sensible significant figures
Correct averaging, percentage-error calculations and polynomial regression consistent with physics
Raw data presentation includes individual trials, means and instrument uncertainties
Processed graphs carry labelled axes, numerical trend-line equations and error bars
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation through the polynomial fit is not performed
Inconsistent reporting of RÂ² (0.90 vs. 0.903) indicates significant-figure discrepancies
Reliance on spreadsheet default rounding introduces minor processing omissions
Minor typographical and formatting slips in figure captions and headers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is strong (band 5), but there are significant omissions in uncertainty consideration and some processing inconsistencies (band 3â4). The work sits at the top of the 3â4 band overall, warranting a score of 4.

strengths: Research question embedded in a specific and appropriate context with clear theoretical grounding
Detailed apparatus list, annotated photos and controlled-variable table that allow reproducibility
Thirteen-step procedure specifying positions, angles, distances and instrument uncertainties
Logical linkage from equations to hypothesis reinforcing the studyâs aim
weaknesses: Use of decimal commas may confuse readers; inconsistent notation
Cloth placement protocol lacks detail on mounting and tension control
Omitted uncertainty values for fan height and table height
Launcher alignment method to zero degrees is not fully specified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (research question context and methodological considerations for data collection) meet the upper band 5â6 criteria, and the third strand (methodological considerations) is solidly at the top of band 3â4. The overall design is specific, reproducible and well contextualized, with only minor ambiguities preventing a perfect 6. A holistic score of 5 accurately reflects this quality.

strengths: Conclusion is justified and fully consistent with the processed data
Quantitative comparison between theoretical and experimental ranges uses percentage error to support validity
Relevant and justified comparison to accepted drag theory and scientific context
Clear discussion linking findings to quadratic drag dependence and Reynolds-number considerations
weaknesses: Nuance about theoretical model choice (e.g. drag coefficient variation) remains underdeveloped
Second percentage-error equation lacks its final numerical result
Notation for horizontal velocity ($v_x$) is not explicitly defined in the derivation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands meet the 5â6 criteria: conclusions are fully supported by analysis and comparisons to scientific context are well justified. Minor undeveloped nuances keep this from a perfect 6, so a score of 5 reflects the criterionâs high but not flawless quality.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses identified and their impacts qualitatively explained
Realistic and relevant improvements proposed, with clear benefits for accuracy and validity
Deep methodological awareness demonstrated (e.g. Reynolds-number critique, turbulence from cloth)
Strong linkage between RÂ² discussion and the dependability of the fit
weaknesses: Quantitative estimates of how each weakness skews results are not provided
Some improvement suggestions would benefit from additional measurement or implementation details
Further expansion of the rationale for certain improvements (e.g. laser rangefinder precision gains) could strengthen the discussion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both evaluation strands align with the top band (5â6): specific weaknesses are explained and realistic improvements are explained. The absence of quantitative impact estimates prevents a full 6, so a score of 5 aptly captures the depth and relevance of the evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated, linked to the measurable quantities (fan speed and projectile range) and embedded in a concrete situation involving a light plastic ball, a fixed launch arrangement and an indoor fan.  Background theory on drag and projectile motion is offered so that the reader understands why the investigation matters.  This constitutes a **specific and appropriate context**, though not every physical parameter (e.g. exact Reynolds-number regime) is fully developed, so the strand sits solidly in but not at the top of the 5-6 range.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted for every device, means include range-based uncertainty estimates and error bars are plotted.  Yet propagation of uncertainty through the polynomial fit is absent and combined uncertainty on the final relationship is not treated.  Thus consideration is **appropriate but with significant omissions**, matching band 3-4 (upper end).

Key methodological issuesâmass of ball, launch velocity stability, fan speed quantisation, cloth induced turbulenceâare identified, and steps such as five repeats and uncertainty listing show awareness of sufficiency.  However, the student mainly **describes** rather than **explains in depth** why each choice secures relevant and *sufficient* data (e.g. no rationale for 5 repeats or 0.02 m range resolution).  This aligns with the 3-4 band, at its top end.

Specific weaknessesâcloth-induced turbulence, motor heat raising air temperature, fan voltage fluctuations, limited speed range, polynomial-fit anomalyâare **explained with commentary on how each could skew results**.  Impact is discussed qualitatively, satisfying the requirements of the highest band (score 5 rather than 6 as no quantitative estimate of impact is offered).

The apparatus list, annotated photographs, controlled-variable table and thirteen-step procedure describe how to collect each data set (five trials at fourteen fan speeds).  Positions, angles, distances and instrument uncertainties are specified, enabling another student to replicate the work with only minor clarifications (e.g. exact launcher alignment method).  Hence the description **allows reproduction** and merits the upper band, though a few small ambiguities (cloth placement protocol) prevent full marks.

Averaging, calculation of percentage errors and polynomial regression are executed correctly; the data trend is consistent with physics.  Minor issuesâno propagation of distance uncertainty into the fit and reliance on spreadsheet default roundingâconstitute **significant but not major omissions**, placing the work in the 3-4 band.

Improvements such as laser rangefinder, automated launcher, voltage stabiliser and wider fan-speed source are **realistic, relevant and their benefits are clearly explained** in terms of accuracy or validity.  The rationale is strong though not mathematically quantified, so a score of 5 within the top band is appropriate.

Raw-data tables include trial values, means and associated instrument uncertainties; processed graphs carry clearly labelled axes and numerical trend-line equations.  Numerical values are presented to sensible significant figures.  Overall, the communication of data is **both clear and precise**, though a few typographical slips appear.

The student links findings to established drag theory (quadratic dependence, ballistic coefficient, Reynolds number) and uses those ideas to explain deviations, demonstrating a **relevant and justified comparison to accepted scientific context**.  Minor simplifications prevent full marks but the work is clearly in the top band.

The conclusion restates the observed inverse relationship and quantitatively compares theoretical and experimental drag-free range, using percentage error to justify validity.  Assertions are **consistent with and directly supported by processed data**; reasoning is explicit, warranting the 5-6 band (score 5 as some nuance about model choice remains undeveloped).

annotations.comment: Decimal commas appear in fan-speed values (e.g. â0,30 msâ»Â¹â) which may confuse readers. Use decimal points consistently (e.g. â0.30 msâ»Â¹â).

The research question is clearly stated and links force and range, but specifying âforceâ singularly (not âforcesâ) would improve precision. Consider refining terminology to match the drag equation context.
The introduction concisely frames the studyâs aim and highlights relevance of drag in projectile motion. This contextual grounding aids reader understanding.
Equations for ideal projectile motion are presented clearly and correctly. LaTeX formatting enhances readability.
The logical link from Equation 1 to the expected decrease in range is well articulated, reinforcing the studyâs hypothesis.
Dependent-variable measurement uncertainty is given in fps. Clarify how frame-rate uncertainty (Â±0.30 fps) translates into spatial uncertainty (Â±0.02 m).
Controlled-variables table is comprehensive but dense. Consider splitting into two tables or adding subheadings to improve readability.
The materials table omits uncertainty for fan height and table height. Add measurement precision for these dimensions to support reproducibility.
Step 1 is clear on setup, but describe how to align the launcher precisely to zero degrees to ensure reproducible positioning.
Using cloth to slow airflow lacks detail on how its tension and distance are controlled. Specify cloth mounting to prevent oscillation.
Figure 4 caption is concise. Add full citation details (author, year) in caption or bibliography to comply with academic convention.
Raw data table is well structured, including individual trials, means, and uncertainties. Communication is both clear and precise.
The multi-row header could be streamlined: separate âFan Speedâ and âDistance Traveledâ into distinct tables or break into clearer sub-headers.
Discussion of fan discrete jump is insightful. Consider quantifying the voltage-speed relationship to strengthen analysis.
Figure 5 caption should match numbering in text (currently Figure 5) and appear in the discussion for better reader guidance.
Good interpretation of quadratic drag dependence. For clarity, split long reasoning into shorter paragraphs.
RÂ² reported as 0.90 but later as 0.903. Ensure consistent significant figures when citing regression metrics.
Prediction of zero-range at 6.99 msâ»Â¹ is insightful; include uncertainty bounds on this extrapolation to indicate confidence limits.
Result table with error bounds clearly summarises max/min ranges, aiding interpretation. Presentation is strong.
The theoretical derivation of horizontal range uses $$s_x=v\sqrt{2h/g}$$ correctly for horizontal launch, but explicitly denote $v_x$ to avoid ambiguity.
Percentage-error calculation is clearly shown but the second equation lacks its numerical result. Add final value for completeness.
Calculated experimental error (2.06 %) is correct and well presented. This strengthens confidence in data validity.
Evaluation strengths highlight strong variable control and sensitivity; commendable depth in linking RÂ² to dependability.
Identification of polynomial-fit anomaly is apt; to enhance, quantify how much the vertex deviation impacts model accuracy.
Inclusion of the Reynoldsânumber equation enhances critical evaluation of drag assumptions.
Discussion of cloth-induced turbulence shows deep methodological awareness and explains how results could be biased.
Thermodynamic impact suggestion is insightful; consider measuring air temperature upstream and downstream of cloth to support claims.
Citation entries are formatted consistently, aiding traceability of sources.
Long URLs break across lines in citations. Consider using DOI links or short URLs to maintain readability.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
